critic_raw: The design uses 'item position in SERP' as a feature for the transformer. This is concerning — if item position is a feature during training, the model learns to use the rank position as a signal. In a production system, the model generates its own rankings, so position would not be a pre-determined feature. Using historical BM25-assigned positions as a feature could cause the model to learn BM25 ranking quality rather than semantic relevance. The design acknowledges this risk for label construction (using IPW), but position as a feature is a separate issue.
defender_raw: The design includes item position in SERP as a feature for the cross-encoder transformer in the context of cross-encoding query-product pairs from historical result lists. Since the evaluation is offline (on historical BM25 result lists), the position feature represents the BM25-assigned rank — the model can learn to use this context signal. However, at inference time in production, if the model is applied to re-rank items from a candidate set, the initial position from BM25 retrieval would be available as a feature. This is a pointwise re-ranking use case where position as a feature is legitimate. The design should clarify that position reflects the initial retrieval rank, not a target variable. The IPW correction ensures evaluation is not dominated by position-click correlation.
adjudication_raw: One concern: item position in SERP as a feature may cause model to learn BM25 quality rather than semantic relevance. Addressed: in a re-ranking scenario, initial BM25 position is a legitimate input feature; IPW corrects position-click correlation in labels. The concern is acknowledged as a design consideration but not a fundamental flaw. Verdict: defense_wins.
